title: Debunking
alt: Mark Zuckerberg has only neutral feelings toward Peppa Pig, who he understands is a fictional character, and he blames the coronavirus pandemic on other factors.
transcript:
[Several news headlines are shown in boxes.]
[Box 1] AP photos show Dr. Fauci's office contains a normal number of microwaves
[Box 2] Fact check: singer Billie Eilish was born years after the TWA Flight 800 explosion
[Box 3] Vaccinated people can remove their hats without trouble by tugging upward, say doctors
[Box 4] Physicists say Dorito powder is affected by gravity
[Box 5] Steering wheels will work normally on Dec 12th; make left and right turns as usual
[Box 6] CNN investigation: Santa's skin is dry and healthy this year, with the same amount of oil as before
[Caption below the panel:]
I don't know whether the "Don't repeat the claim in the headline debunking it" thing works or not, but it definitely makes reading the news weird.
explanation:
When writing a news article that "debunks" a claim (shows why it is false), writing its headline in the form "X is false" is discouraged. The reason is that just repeatedly seeing "X", even if negated or followed by "is false", can make readers subconsciously believe it.

To avoid this, Randall as a journalist has worded his debunking articles in a positive sense. This makes for a confusing read if the reader has not heard of the original claim. The "original claims" allegedly being debunked here don't actually appear to have been made anywhere, and can only be inferred from the debunking. 

Much of the debunking relies on setting simple facts straight, making for bizarrely banal headlines.  

===Table===

Article Headline                                                                                                                                                              	Possible claim being debunked
AP photos show that Dr. Fauci's office contains a normal number of microwaves                                                                                                 	That Dr. 's office is subject to excessive microwaves. In 2021, Fauci was the head of the  and chief medical advisor to President Trump. He was seen as the public face to the COVID-19 response in that country. 

An early conspiracy theory proposed that newly-installed 5G towers for mobile network access were emitting harmful "microwave" radiation that caused the infections. Conversely, a study concluded that heating food in microwave ovens was an excellent method of deactivating such a type of coronavirus.

US diplomats have been altering their lives due to rumors of a , which are unexplained health problems and symptoms that some blamed on a hypothetical microwave weapon of unknown design. Microwave radiation is blamed by a number of conspiracy theories for health problems or manipulation of the public, giving rise to the  meme.

However, because the headline references photographs to debunk the claim, the news article is apparently talking about the number of microwave ovens in his office. Either there is some miscommunication between the conspiracy theorists and debunkers, or the original claimant was fixated on Fauci's office appliances for some reason. Or they thought Fauci really liked microwave ovens.

The vague phrasing also implies that the number of microwaves pictured may be larger than most people would consider "normal" for an office, and that the article is defending that large number as "normal". Since microwaves are not typical office equipment, most offices will only have one or two microwaves in the break room, though someone with a private office (as Dr. Fauci has) might bring one of their own to have more privacy while eating.
Fact check: Singer Billie Eilish was born years after the TWA Flight 800 explosion                                                                                            	A link between  (born December 2001) and the  crash in July 1996. No malicious or terrorist activity was found by the investigation, but  in its aftermath. Eilish's lyrics and videos often explore death and macabre themes, such as a prominent "fallen angel" motif. Eilish was only 14 years old when she began her career and has been well-known as a child star, so this link is shown to be particularly absurd.
Vaccinated people can remove their hats without trouble by tugging upward, say doctors                                                                                        	Vaccination has long been the subject of many, many conspiracy theories about potential side effects; this article could be commenting on any number of such, from claims that vaccines magnetize people (popular in June 2021), that vaccines cause one's head to swell thus making hats become tight, that vaccination makes one's head sticky or fuse to one's hat, that vaccination causes hats to become tighter, that vaccination causes one's inability to raise one's arm high enough to tug one's hat upward.
Physicists say Dorito powder is affected by gravity                                                                                                                           	A claim that  powder is not affected by gravity. For example, an anecdote around a local businessman discovering that objects levitate when sufficient powder is applied to them, or people misunderstanding dust floating under atmospheric turbulence as evidence of "antigravity".
Steering wheels will work normally on Dec 12th; make left and right turns as usual                                                                                            	Faults (perhaps due to failures in the power steering system), triggered for example by solar storms, latent software bugs, a deliberate software hack, or some unknown phenomenon, will cause cars to steer erratically on that date. Superstition may be blamed for such rumors, or deliberate malice, inducing people to reverse their steering gestures and lose control.
CNN investigation: Santa's skin is dry and healthy this year, with the same amount of oil as before                                                                           	is suffering from oily skin, which can cause acne. Skin conditions may be caused by a diet of whisky, cookies and milk, causing parents to feel guilty for leaving treats on the fireplace overnight. CNN appears to tacitly reveal that Santa Claus is a real person, or just keeping up the  for children, in the tradition of "".
(Title text) Mark Zuckerberg has only neutral feelings toward Peppa Pig, who he understands is a fictional character, and he blames the coronavirus pandemic on other factors.	That the founder of Facebook () has strongly negative feelings toward the cartoon character , believing her to be a real talking pig and the cause of the COVID-19 pandemic, or maybe that he is a fan with some out-of-control behaviors towards "her". May be a reference to a recent speech by UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson in which he expressed admiration for Peppa, much to the bewilderment of journalists.
